questions.text: When the antinodes, in stationary waves, are passing through their equilibrium position, the energy is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: As an object undergoes SHM, such as a mass-spring system or a simple pendulum, its kinetic energy and potential energy continuously transform from one form to the other. At the equilibrium position, where the displacement is zero, the kinetic energy is maximized, as the object reaches its maximum velocity. Simultaneously, the potential energy is at its minimum, as the displacement from the equilibrium position is zero. As the object moves away from the equilibrium, the kinetic energy decreases, reaching zero at the extremum positions, where the potential energy is maximized. The potential energy stored in the system is then converted back into kinetic energy as the object returns towards the equilibrium position

Stationary waves do not cause any energy transfer but the particles present contain energy. Particles at antinode have maximum velocity and hence maximum kinetic energy while crossing the equilibrium position, while potential energy is zero so the energy is wholly kinetic. When the antinodes are all at their extreme displacements, the energy stored is wholly potential.

options.text: A combination of both forms of energy
options.explanation: As an object undergoes SHM, such as a mass-spring system or a simple pendulum, its kinetic energy and potential energy continuously transform from one form to the other. At the equilibrium position, where the displacement is zero, the kinetic energy is maximized, as the object reaches its maximum velocity. Simultaneously, the potential energy is at its minimum, as the displacement from the equilibrium position is zero. As the object moves away from the equilibrium, the kinetic energy decreases, reaching zero at the extremum positions, where the potential energy is maximized. The potential energy stored in the system is then converted back into kinetic energy as the object returns towards the equilibrium position

options.text: There is no energy in stationary waves
options.explanation: As an object undergoes SHM, such as a mass-spring system or a simple pendulum, its kinetic energy and potential energy continuously transform from one form to the other. At the equilibrium position, where the displacement is zero, the kinetic energy is maximized, as the object reaches its maximum velocity. Simultaneously, the potential energy is at its minimum, as the displacement from the equilibrium position is zero. As the object moves away from the equilibrium, the kinetic energy decreases, reaching zero at the extremum positions, where the potential energy is maximized. The potential energy stored in the system is then converted back into kinetic energy as the object returns towards the equilibrium position

questions.explanation: There is no energy transfer in stationary waves, but the energy of the antinodes keeps on changing from potential to kinetic energy. At the complete stretched position, the energy is wholly potential but as it passes through the equilibrium position, it is kinetic only.

questions.text: The beat frequency is given by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Beat is produced when two waves of nearby frequencies superimpose when they travel in the same path. This causes a periodic variation of intensity of the resultant wave.The beat frequency is the number of beats produced per second.The formula for beat frequency is the difference in frequency of the two superimposed waves.

Beat is produced when two waves of nearby frequencies superimpose when they travel in the same path. This causes a periodic variation of intensity of the resultant wave.The beat frequency is the number of beats produced per second.The formula for beat frequency is the difference in frequency of the two superimposed waves.

Beat is produced when two waves of nearby frequencies superimpose when they travel in the same path. This causes a periodic variation of intensity of the resultant wave.The beat frequency is the number of beats produced per second.The formula for beat frequency is the difference in frequency of the two superimposed waves.

Beat is produced when two waves of nearby frequencies superimpose when they travel in the same path. This causes a periodic variation of intensity of the resultant wave.The beat frequency is the number of beats produced per second.The formula for beat frequency is the difference in frequency of the two superimposed waves.

questions.explanation: The beat frequency is the difference in frequency between two interacting waves. If you have two waves with frequencies f1 and f2, the beat frequency can be calculated using the formula:f= f1 - f2 This formula is commonly used in physics and signal processing when dealing with the interference or interaction of waves, such as in the case of two sound waves or two electromagnetic waves. The beat frequency represents the frequency at which the amplitude of the resulting wave (obtained by superimposing the two original waves) varies, creating a pulsating pattern..

questions.text: A traveling wave, in which the particles of the disturbed medium move perpendicular to the direction of propagation of the wave is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The type of wave you're referring to is called a transverse wave. In a transverse wave, the particles of the medium oscillate perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. This means that while the wave moves in one direction, the individual particles move up and down or side to side. Examples of transverse waves include electromagnetic waves (such as light and radio waves) and waves on a string or water surface.
In this wave motion, the particles travel along the direction of wave movement.
In a standing wave, energy does not flow.
A shock wave is a strong pressure wave in any elastic medium such as air, water, or a solid substance, produced by supersonic aircraft.

questions.explanation: Longitudinal waves are those waves in which particles of the disturbed medium move parallel to the direction of propagation of the wave. Transverse waves are those waves in which the particles of the disturbed medium move perpendicular to the direction of progagation of the wave. A shock wave is considered to be a longitudinal wave and a standing waves are not progressive or travelling waves as they do not transfer energy.

questions.text: A ship approaching a seaport at 10 km/h sends a sonic signal of frequency of 1000 Hz. What is the apparent frequency of the signal as received by a receiver on the seaport?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect as per the formula and calculations
This option is incorrect as per the formula and calculations
Frequency = 1000 HzSpeed of ship = 10 km/h = 2.78 m/sSonic signal speed = 340 m/sApparent frequency is found as:f’ = vsound / (vsound - vship) x ff’ = 340 / (340 - 2.78) x 1000f’ = 1008 Hz
This option is incorrect as per the formula and calculations

questions.explanation: Frequency = 1000 HzSpeed of ship = 10 km/h = 2.78 m/sSonic signal speed = 340 m/sApparent frequency is found as:f’ = vsound / (vsound - vship) x ff’ = 340 / (340 - 2.78) x 1000f’ = 1008 Hz

questions.text: If a wave can be polarized, it means, it is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a longitudinal wave, the oscillations occur parallel to the direction of wave propagation.
A stationary wave is formed by the superposition of two waves traveling in opposite directions with the same frequency and amplitude.
A superimposed wave refers to the combination of two or more waves that are added together to form a resultant wave.
Transverse wave, as polarization is a characteristic that can only be exhibited by transverse waves.

questions.explanation: Only transverse waves can be polarised. Longitudinal waves cannot be polarised.Transverse: vibrations perpendicular to the direction of travel of the wave.Longitudinal: vibrations parallel to the direction of travel of the wave.Sound waves are longitudinal so they cannot be polarised.Polarised: the wave vibrations occur in one plane onlyUnpolarised: the wave vibrations occur in all planes perpendicular to the direction of travel.The reason that only transverse waves can be polarised is that their vibrations can potentially occur in all directions perpendicular to the direction of travel. It is therefore possible to confine the vibrations to a single plane. Or put another way an unpolarised transverse wave is a 3D shape, it is therefore possible to confine it to a 2D shape (a plane). The vibrations of a longitudinal wave occur along a single line, it is therefore not possible to confine that to a plane, i.e. a 1D shape cannot be confined within a 2D shape. Similarly, Stationary waves and superimposed waves can not be polarised as they are along a single line.

questions.text: In Doppler effect, if listener moves towards a stationary source then:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Observed frequency is greater than original frequency
options.explanation: In the Doppler effect, if the listener moves toward the stationary source, the observed frequency is greater than the original frequency.

options.text: Observed frequency is less than original frequency
options.explanation: This option would be true if the listener were moving away from the stationary source as the distance would increase, causing the waves to be stretched out.

options.text: Observed frequency is equal to original frequency
options.explanation: This option would be true if there were no relative motion between the listener and the stationary source as the distance would be constant.

options.text: Observed frequency is independent of original frequency
options.explanation: This option is not accurate because the observed frequency is directly influenced by the original frequency and the relative motion between the listener and the source.

questions.explanation: As the listener moves towards a stationary source, the apparent frequency heard by the listener increases, while the original frequency of the source remains constant. This is known as a "blue shift" in the context of visible light, where light from a source moving towards an observer appears shifted towards the blue end of the spectrum.

questions.text: A shock wave is produced due to an earthquake which makes the buildings move in the direction of the shock wave. Which progressive wave would this be?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A longitudinal wave is characterized by particle displacement parallel to the direction of wave propagation. In the case of a shock wave from an earthquake, the ground and buildings move in the same direction as the wave, which is a defining characteristic of longitudinal waves.

A transverse wave involves particle movement perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. While some seismic waves can be transverse, the specific wave mentioned in the context (shock wave) is primarily longitudinal.

A material wave typically refers to waves that propagate through a medium due to the medium's physical properties. However, this term is not specific enough to describe the type of wave produced by an earthquake shock wave.

A particle wave is not a standard term used in wave mechanics. It does not accurately describe the nature of waves like shock waves, which are defined based on the movement of energy through a medium.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Longitudinal wave because shock waves, such as those produced during an earthquake, cause the particles in the medium (like the ground) to oscillate in the same direction as the wave travels. This parallel movement of particles is characteristic of longitudinal waves. In contrast, Transverse waves have particle motion perpendicular to the wave direction, which does not apply to shock waves in this context. The term Material wave is too vague and does not specifically describe seismic waves, while Particle wave is not a recognized category in wave physics and does not provide a meaningful description of any wave type.

questions.text: A wave, which transfers _ by moving away from the source of disturbance is called _ wave.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A wave does not carry momentum.
A Standing wave does not carry energy.

A progressive wave carries energy but not medium.A wave, which transfers energy by moving away from the source of disturbance, is called a "propagating" wave.In general, waves are characterized by their ability to transfer energy from one location to another without transferring matter. When a wave propagates, it means that it travels through a medium or through space, carrying energy along with it.There are different types of waves, such as mechanical waves (e.g., sound waves, water waves) and electromagnetic waves (e.g., light waves, radio waves). These waves can be further classified based on their specific characteristics, such as whether they require a medium to propagate (mechanical waves) or can propagate through a vacuum (electromagnetic waves).So, in summary, a wave that transfers energy by moving away from the source of disturbance is called a "propagating" wave.
A wave does not carry momentum.

questions.explanation: Progressive waves are waves that carry energy away from the source as they propagate through a medium. These waves transfer energy from one point to another without transporting matter. As progressive waves move through the medium, the particles of the medium oscillate and transfer energy along the direction of the wave.

questions.text: If the amplitude is 200, the intensity is 300. When the amplitude is increased to 800, then what will be the intensity?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: As per the solution, this option is incorrect.
Incorrect as per formula
Incorrect as per formula
Incorrect as per formula

We know that The intensity of a wave is directly proportional to the square of its amplitude Mathematically-> I x a2HereI = intensitya = amplitudeAccording to the question, we are asked to find the new intensity given that If the amplitude is 200, the intensity is 300. When the amplitude is increased to 800HenceI = 300a = 200a' = 800Increase in amplitude (A)-> A = a'/aHence-> A = 800/200On further simplificationWe get-> A = 4Increase in amplitude (squared)-> A2 = 42-> A2 = 16Amplitude squared increases by a factor of 16-> I' = I x A2HereI' = new intensityI = initial intensityA² = squared amplitude factorIf the amplitude is 200, the intensity is 300. When the amplitude is increased to 800.HenceI = 300 We found out thatA² = 16Substituting the values-> I' = 300 x 16On further simplification-> I' = 4800HenceNew intensity = 4800When the amplitude is increased to 800 then the intensity will be 4800.

questions.explanation: We know that The intensity of a wave is directly proportional to the square of its amplitude Mathematically-> I x a2HereI = intensitya = amplitudeAccording to the question, we are asked to find the new intensity given that If the amplitude is 200, the intensity is 300. When the amplitude is increased to 800HenceI = 300a = 200a' = 800Increase in amplitude (A)-> A = a'/aHence-> A = 800/200On further simplificationWe get-> A = 4Increase in amplitude (squared)-> A2 = 42-> A2 = 16Amplitude squared increases by a factor of 16-> I' = I x A2HereI' = new intensityI = initial intensityA² = squared amplitude factorIf the amplitude is 200, the intensity is 300. When the amplitude is increased to 800.HenceI = 300 We found out thatA² = 16Substituting the values-> I' = 300 x 16On further simplification-> I' = 4800HenceNew intensity = 4800When the amplitude is increased to 800 then the intensity will be 4800.

questions.text: A special class of waves that does not need a material medium for their propagation are called
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Electromagnetic waves are the type of waves that do not require a material medium for their propagation, and they include electric and magnetic components.Electromagnetic waves encompass a broad spectrum, including radio waves, microwaves, infrared waves, visible light, ultraviolet waves, X-rays, and gamma rays. These waves are characterized by oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other and to the direction of wave propagation.

rather than "magnetic waves," the more accurate term to describe waves that do not require a material medium is "electromagnetic waves," emphasizing both the electric and magnetic components of the oscillating fields.

Electromagnetic waves are the type of waves that do not require a material medium for their propagation, and they include electric and magnetic components.Electromagnetic waves encompass a broad spectrum, including radio waves, microwaves, infrared waves, visible light, ultraviolet waves, X-rays, and gamma rays. These waves are characterized by oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other and to the direction of wave propagation.

Sound waves do require a material medium for their propagation. They are a type of mechanical wave, and their transmission involves the vibration of particles in a material medium, such as air, water, or solids.

All these seismic waves are mechanical waves and require a material medium to propagate. They travel through the Earth, causing it to shake during an earthquake. They cannot propagate through a vacuum.

questions.explanation: Electromagnetic waves do not require a medium for travel since they are not transmitted by the vibration of particles. The waves are generated by the oscillation of electric and magnetic fields which can exist anywhere.Electric waves do not isolatedly exist rather they depend on the magnetic field to form EM waves.A sound wave is a pattern of disturbance caused by the movement of energy traveling through a medium (such as air, water, or any other liquid or solid ).Seismic waves are usually generated by movements of the Earth's tectonic plates but may also be caused by explosions, volcanoes, and landslides. When an earthquake occurs shockwaves of energy, called seismic waves, are released from the earthquake focus.A changing magnetic field will induce a changing electric field and vice-versa—the two are linked. These changing fields form electromagnetic waves. Electromagnetic waves differ from mechanical waves in that they do not require a medium to propagate.

questions.text: A sound wave with a frequency of 343 Hz travels through the air. What is its wavelength? (speed of sound through air = 343 m/s)

options.explanation: As per the solution, this is an incorrect option.
As per the solution, this is an incorrect option.
As per the solution, this is an incorrect option.
As per the solution, this is an incorrect option.

questions.explanation: λ The speed of a wave v is related to its frequency f and wavelength λ by the formula: v = fλIn this case, the speed of sound v through the air is given as 343 m/s, and the frequency f of the sound wave is also 343 Hz. We can rearrange the formula to solve for the wavelength λλ= v/fSubstitute the given values:λ= 343/343λ= 1m So, the wavelength of the sound wave is 1 meter.

questions.text: Which one of the following properties is not exhibited by the longitudinal waves?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In longitudinal waves, such as sound waves, the particles of the medium oscillate back and forth parallel to the direction of wave propagation. When two or more longitudinal waves overlap, their compressions and rarefactions can add up or cancel out, resulting in interference patterns.

In longitudinal waves, such as sound waves, can exhibit reflection. Reflection is the phenomenon where a wave encounters a boundary or obstacle and bounces back in the opposite direction. The law of reflection states that the angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection.

In longitudinal waves, including sound waves, can exhibit diffraction. Diffraction is the bending of waves around obstacles or the spreading of waves when they encounter an aperture or opening. While diffraction is often more noticeable in waves with longer wavelengths compared to the size of the obstacle or opening, it can occur in both longitudinal and transverse waves.

Longitudinal waves, such as sound waves, do not exhibit polarization in the same way as transverse waves. Polarization is a phenomenon related to the orientation of oscillations in a wave, and it is more characteristic of transverse waves.

questions.explanation: In a transverse wave, the oscillations of the particles occur perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. This allows for the possibility of aligning the vibrations in a specific direction, a property known as polarization.In contrast, longitudinal waves involve oscillations of particles parallel to the direction of wave propagation. Because of this parallel oscillation, longitudinal waves do not exhibit polarization. Sound waves, which are longitudinal waves, do not have a preferred direction for particle oscillations.

questions.text: Astronomers calculate speed of distant stars and galaxies using which of the following phenomena:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Astronomers typically do not use beats to calculate the speed of distant stars and galaxies. Beats refer to the phenomenon that occurs when two waves with slightly different frequencies overlap, producing an oscillating pattern of alternating constructive and destructive interference.

Interference is not commonly used by astronomers to calculate the speed of distant stars and galaxies. The primary method employed for determining the motion of celestial objects, especially those at vast distances, is the Doppler shift.

The superposition principle is a fundamental concept in physics and wave theory, but it is not typically the primary method used by astronomers to calculate the speed of distant stars and galaxies. The superposition principle states that, in a linear medium, the net response at a given point caused by multiple independent waves is the sum of the responses that each wave would produce individually.

In astronomy, the Doppler shift is a more commonly used method for determining the speed of distant celestial objects. The Doppler shift involves measuring the change in frequency or wavelength of electromagnetic waves (such as light) emitted by celestial objects. This shift is caused by the relative motion between the object and the observer.

questions.explanation: The Doppler effect is a phenomenon where the observed frequency of a wave changes depending on the relative motion between the source of the wave and the observer. This effect is commonly used in astronomy to determine the motion of celestial objects, such as stars and galaxies.In the context of astronomy, the most common application of the Doppler effect is the redshift or blueshift of light. When a celestial object is moving away from an observer, its light is shifted towards longer wavelengths (redshift), and when it is moving towards the observer, the light is shifted towards shorter wavelengths (blueshift).Astronomers can measure these shifts in the spectrum of light emitted by stars or galaxies to determine their radial velocity (motion along the line of sight). This information is crucial for understanding the dynamics of galaxies, the expansion of the universe, and other aspects of celestial motion and structure. The redshift of light from distant galaxies played a key role in the discovery of the expansion of the universe.

questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Joule per second is the unit of power, which measures energy transfer over time, not intensity.
This is the correct unit for intensity of sound, as it describes power per unit area.
This combination is not a standard unit for any physical quantity.
Joule per metre square describes energy density, not sound intensity.
Joule metre is not a recognized unit for any conventional physical quantity.

questions.explanation: Intensity is the amount of sound power passing through a unit area, and is measured in watts per square meter (W/m²). The correct answer is Option B: Watt per metre square. Other options do not represent intensity: Option A, Joule per second, measures power; Option C, Watt metre square, is not a recognized unit; Option D, Joule per metre square, measures energy density; and Option E, Joule metre, is not a standard physical unit.

questions.text: A train is approaching a station at 90 km/h sounding a whistle of frequency 1000 Hz. What will be the apparent frequency heard by the listener sitting on the platform if the train moves away from the station at the same speed? (speed of sound= 340 m/s)
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is correct. The Doppler effect formula for a source moving towards a stationary observer gives an apparent frequency of 931.5 Hz.
This is incorrect. This frequency would be much lower than expected for a source moving at 90 km/h.
This is incorrect. This frequency does not result from a train moving at the given speed.
This is incorrect. This frequency is not consistent with the Doppler effect for the given scenario.
This is incorrect. This frequency is not calculated using the correct Doppler effect formula.

questions.explanation: To determine the apparent frequency heard by the listener, we use the Doppler effect formula:f' = f * (v + v_o) / (v + v_s)Here, f is the original frequency (1000 Hz), v is the speed of sound (340 m/s), v_o is the speed of the observer (0 m/s, as the observer is stationary), and v_s is the speed of the source (converted to 25 m/s from 90 km/h).Applying these values: f' = 1000 * (340 / (340 + 25)) = 931.5 Hz.The other options result from incorrect application of the Doppler effect formula, either due to incorrect speed conversions or misunderstanding the direction of motion, leading to incorrect frequency values.

questions.text: The six strings of a guitar are the same length under nearly the same tension, but they have different thicknesses. On which string do waves travel the fastest?

options.explanation: The velocity of a transverse wave in a stretched string is given by: v = √(T/n). Where T is the Tension and n is the mass per unit lenthn = m/L = ρπr^2/L where r is the radius of the wirer = d/2 where d is the diameterSo, v = √ (4T/ρπd^2)v= 2/d √(T/ρπ)As it can be seen, v is inversely proportional to dThus, the velocity of wave is highest for thinnest string.

questions.text: Trough of a wave acts as:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Convex Lens:Shape: Thicker in the middle and curves outwards.Effect on Light: Causes parallel rays of light to converge (come together) after refraction to a focal point.Why it's NOT analogous: The trough of a wave has the opposite curvature to a convex lens. Therefore, it would not cause light to converge. The crest (highest point) of a wave, with its outward curvature, would be analogous to a convex lens.

Convex Mirror:Shape: Reflecting surface curves outwards.Effect on Light: Causes parallel rays of light to diverge after reflection. It forms virtual, upright, and diminished images.Why it's NOT analogous: A trough of a wave is a transmitting medium (light passes through it), not a reflecting surface like a mirror. While both a convex mirror and a wave trough cause divergence, the mechanism is different (reflection vs. refraction).

Plane Mirror:Shape: Flat reflecting surface.Effect on Light: Causes parallel rays of light to reflect parallel to each other. It forms virtual, upright, and same-sized images.Why it's NOT analogous: Again, a trough of a wave is a transmitting medium, not a reflecting surface. A flat surface on a wave would behave differently than a curved trough in terms of how it affects light passing through it.

questions.explanation: The trough of a wave is the lowest point on the wave.A concave lens is a lens having one of both surfaces curved inwards. They are also known as diverging lens and cause the spread of light passing through it. The highest position on the wave is called the crest, which acts as a convex lens. Water waves can be used to demonstrate this phenomenon. The water acts as a lens when light is passed through it. On white paper, the crest and trough are shown as alternate bright and dark lines respectively.

questions.text: When a source is moving towards a stationary observer, the apparent change in frequency will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The apparent change in frequency will be greater than the original frequency when a source is moving towards a stationary observer. This is because the waves from the source are compressed as they approach the observer, which increases the frequency of the waves.The opposite is true when the source is moving away from the observer. In that case, the waves are stretched out, which decreases the frequency of the waves.This is known as the Doppler effect. It is a phenomenon that occurs when there is a relative motion between a source of waves and an observer. The Doppler effect can be observed with sound waves, light waves, and other waves.

options.text: Remains the same as that of original frequency
options.explanation: This is not possible. The frequency of the waves from the source will always change when there is a relative motion between the source and the observer. The only way that the frequency of the waves would remain the same is if the source and the observer were moving at the same speed in the same direction.

This is possible. When a source is moving away from a stationary observer, the waves from the source are stretched out as they move away from the observer, which decreases the frequency of the waves. This is known as the redshift. The redshift is a phenomenon that occurs when there is a relative motion between a source of waves and an observer, and the source is moving away from the observer. The redshift can be observed with light waves, and it is used to measure the expansion of the universe.

This is not possible. The frequency of the waves from the source will always change when there is a relative motion between the source and the observer. The only way that the frequency of the waves would not change is if the source and the observer were not moving relative to each other.

questions.explanation: Option A: The apparent change in frequency will be greater than the original frequency when a source is moving towards a stationary observer. This is because the waves from the source are compressed as they approach the observer, which increases the frequency of the waves.The opposite is true when the source is moving away from the observer. In that case, the waves are stretched out, which decreases the frequency of the waves.This is known as the Doppler effect. It is a phenomenon that occurs when there is a relative motion between a source of waves and an observer. The Doppler effect can be observed with sound waves, light waves, and other waves. Option B: This is not possible. The frequency of the waves from the source will always change when there is a relative motion between the source and the observer. The only way that the frequency of the waves would remain the same is if the source and the observer were moving at the same speed in the same direction. Optio C: This is possible. When a source is moving away from a stationary observer, the waves from the source are stretched out as they move away from the observer, which decreases the frequency of the waves. This is known as the redshift. The redshift is a phenomenon that occurs when there is a relative motion between a source of waves and an observer, and the source is moving away from the observer. The redshift can be observed with light waves, and it is used to measure the expansion of the universe. Option D: This is not possible. The frequency of the waves from the source will always change when there is a relative motion between the source and the observer. The only way that the frequency of the waves would not change is if the source and the observer were not moving relative to each other.

questions.text: If the waves interfere constructively, the amplitude of the resulting wave would be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Less than either of the individual waves
options.explanation: The amplitude of the resulting wave can never be less than either of the individual waves. This is because the crests of the two waves cannot cancel each other out completely. Even if the two waves are out of phase by 180 degrees, the troughs of the two waves will still add together to form a wave with some amplitude.

options.text: Greater than either of the individual waves
options.explanation: The amplitude of the resulting wave would be greater than either of the individual waves. When two waves interfere constructively, the crests of the two waves line up, resulting in a wave with an amplitude that is equal to the sum of the amplitudes of the two individual waves. For example, if two waves with amplitudes of 1 cm and 2 cm interfere constructively, the amplitude of the resulting wave will be 3 cm.

options.text: Equal to the shortest of the individual waves
options.explanation: The amplitude of the resulting wave can also never be equal to the shortest of the individual waves. This is because the crests of the two waves cannot be perfectly aligned if the waves have different amplitudes.

options.text: Equal to the greatest of the individual waves
options.explanation: The amplitude of the resulting wave can be equal to the greatest of the individual waves. This happens when the two waves have the same amplitude and are in phase.

questions.explanation: Option A: The amplitude of the resulting wave can never be less than either of the individual waves. This is because the crests of the two waves cannot cancel each other out completely. Even if the two waves are out of phase by 180 degrees, the troughs of the two waves will still add together to form a wave with some amplitude. Option B: The amplitude of the resulting wave would be greater than either of the individual waves. When two waves interfere constructively, the crests of the two waves line up, resulting in a wave with an amplitude that is equal to the sum of the amplitudes of the two individual waves. For example, if two waves with amplitudes of 1 cm and 2 cm interfere constructively, the amplitude of the resulting wave will be 3 cm. Option C: The amplitude of the resulting wave can also never be equal to the shortest of the individual waves. This is because the crests of the two waves cannot be perfectly aligned if the waves have different amplitudes. Option D: The amplitude of the resulting wave can be equal to the greatest of the individual waves. This happens when the two waves have the same amplitude and are in phase.

questions.text: If a compressible medium has bulk modulus denoted by B and density denoted by P, then a Newton's formula for the speed of sound in a medium is _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct expression for the speed of sound, derived from the properties of bulk modulus and density.
This is incorrect because multiplying bulk modulus and density does not give the speed of sound. The correct relationship involves division under the square root.
This is incorrect because the speed of sound is not a simple ratio of bulk modulus to density. The correct formula involves a square root.
This is incorrect because it inverts the relationship between bulk modulus and density. The bulk modulus should be in the numerator under the square root.

questions.explanation: The speed of sound in a compressible medium is given by Newton's formula: V = √(B / P), where B is the bulk modulus and P is the density of the medium. The formula shows that the speed of sound is directly proportional to the square root of the ratio of the bulk modulus to the density. This is because a higher bulk modulus (indicating a stiffer medium) and a lower density both contribute to a higher speed of sound.Option A is correct because it correctly reflects this relationship. Option B is incorrect as it suggests multiplying the bulk modulus by the density, which does not describe the speed of sound. Option C is incorrect because it lacks the square root, which is necessary for the correct calculation. Option D is incorrect as it reverses the roles of bulk modulus and density.

questions.text: Laplace's correction to Newton’s formula is based on the fact that the compressions and rarefactions occur as _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Laplace's correction introduces the adiabatic bulk modulus, accounting for temperature changes during compressions and rarefactions, making the process adiabatic.
Newton assumed isothermal conditions, but this ignored the rapid nature of sound waves where temperature changes occur, making this assumption incorrect.
An isochoric process implies constant volume, which is not applicable to the compressions and rarefactions in sound waves.
An isobaric process implies constant pressure, which does not occur in the dynamic changes of sound wave propagation.

questions.explanation: Laplace's correction to Newton’s formula recognizes that sound waves involve rapid compressions and rarefactions where there is insufficient time for heat exchange with surroundings, making the process adiabatic. Newton's original assumption of an isothermal process (constant temperature) was incorrect because it fails to consider the rapid temperature changes accompanying pressure variations in sound waves.The adiabatic process assumption accounts for these temperature changes, leading to a more accurate formula for sound speed in gases. The other processes—isochoric (constant volume) and isobaric (constant pressure)—do not describe the conditions experienced during sound wave propagation.

Therefore, the full wavelength can be found by doubling this distance. 15 x 2= 30m

questions.text: The frequency of a string on a musical instrument can be changed either by:

options.text: Varying the tension or by changing the thickness

options.explanation: Varying the tension leads to increase or decrease in frequency, while changing the length of the string also changes the frequency.

options.text: Varying the thickness or by changing the length

questions.explanation: Varying the tension leads to increase or decrease in frequency, while changing the length of the string also changes the frequency.

questions.text: The stationary waves can be set up on the string only with the frequencies of harmonic series determined by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The tension, length and mass per unit length of the string
options.explanation: The following is the solution:The frequencies of the harmonic series on a string are determined by the tension (T) and mass per unit length (μ) of the string, in addition to the length of the string (L).The frequencies of the harmonic series on a string can be calculated using the following formula:fₙ = (1 / 2L) * √(T / μ) * nwhere:fₙ is the frequency of the nth harmonic,L is the length of the string,T is the tension in the string, andμ is the mass per unit length of the string.The term √(T / μ) represents the wave speed (v) on the string. Therefore, the formula can also be written as:fₙ = (n / 2L) * vSo, to summarise, the frequencies of the harmonic series on a string are determined by the length of the string (L), the tension in the string (T), and the mass per unit length of the string (μ).

options.text: The tension and mass per unit length of the string only
options.explanation: Frequencies of harmonic series cannot be determined by tension and mass per unit length only.

options.text: The length and mass per unit length of the string only
options.explanation: Frequencies of harmonic series cannot be determined by length and mass per unit length only.

options.text: The tension and length of the string only
options.explanation: Frequencies of harmonic series cannot be determined by tension and length only.

questions.explanation: The following is the solution: The frequencies of the harmonic series on a string are determined by the length of the string (L), the tension in the string (T), and the mass per unit length of the string (μ). The frequencies of the harmonic series on a string can be calculated using the following formula:fₙ = (1 / 2L) * √(T / μ) * nwhere:fₙ is the frequency of the nth harmonic,L is the length of the string,T is the tension in the string, andμ is the mass per unit length of the string.The term √(T / μ) represents the wave speed (v) on the string. Therefore, the formula can also be written as:fₙ = (n / 2L) * vSo, to summarise, the frequencies of the harmonic series on a string are determined by the length of the string (L), the tension in the string (T), and the mass per unit length of the string (μ).
hints: set up on the string only

questions.text: If a pipe is closed at one end and open at the other, the closed end is a:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: An antinode is a point of maximum amplitude in a standing wave. In a closed-open pipe, the open end acts as an antinode because the air particles at the open end can move freely, allowing for maximum displacement and pressure variation. At the open end, the amplitude of the standing wave is at its maximum.

A node is a point of zero amplitude in a standing wave. In a closed-open pipe, the closed end acts as a node because the air particles at the closed end cannot move, resulting in minimal displacement and pressure variation. At the closed end, the amplitude of the standing wave is at its minimum.

Rarefaction refers to the region in a longitudinal wave where the density of the medium is less than its equilibrium value. In the context of a closed-open pipe, rarefaction occurs in the regions of minimum pressure variation, which are typically associated with nodes. Therefore, rarefaction is not directly related to the closed end of the pipe.

A crest is the highest point of a wave. In a standing wave, the points of maximum amplitude, such as antinodes, can be considered as crests. However, the closed end of a closed-open pipe does not act as a crest because it corresponds to a point of minimum displacement and pressure variation.

questions.explanation: A node is a point of zero amplitude in a standing wave. In a closed-open pipe, the closed end acts as a node because the air particles at the closed end cannot move, resulting in minimal displacement and pressure variation. At the closed end, the amplitude of the standing wave is at its minimum.
hints: pipe is closed at one end

questions.text: When a standing wave is set up on a string fixed at both ends, which of the following statements is true?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Sum of the number of antinodes and the number of nodes is always even
options.explanation: A. The sum of the number of antinodes and the number of nodes is not always even. The sum depends on the specific standing wave pattern and the mode of vibration. In some cases, it may be even, but in other cases, it may not.

options.text: Wavelength = length string / number of nodes
options.explanation: B. The wavelength of a standing wave on a string is not equal to the length of the string divided by the number of nodes. The wavelength is determined by the distance between two adjacent nodes or two adjacent antinodes.

options.text: The shape of the string at any instant shows a symmetry about the midpoint of the string
options.explanation: When a standing wave is set up on a string fixed at both ends, the correct statement is that the shape of the string at any instant shows symmetry about the midpoint of the string. This means that the displacement of the string from its equilibrium position is the same on either side of the midpoint.When a standing wave is set up on a string fixed at both ends, the following statement is true:A. The nodes are points of minimum displacement, and the antinodes are points of maximum displacement.In a standing wave on a string fixed at both ends, certain points on the string remain stationary while others oscillate with varying amplitudes. These stationary points are called nodes, and the points with maximum displacement are called antinodes.The standing wave is formed due to the superposition of two waves traveling in opposite directions along the string. The interference of these waves leads to constructive and destructive interference at different points on the string.At the nodes, the two waves interfere destructively, resulting in zero displacement. This means the string does not move at the nodes, and they represent points of minimum displacement.At the antinodes, the two waves interfere constructively, leading to the maximum displacement of the string. The amplitude of the oscillations is highest at the antinodes, making them points of maximum displacement.The standing wave pattern has a fixed pattern of nodes and antinodes, depending on the mode of vibration (fundamental mode or higher harmonics). The specific arrangement of nodes and antinodes on the string is determined by the wavelength of the standing wave and the length of the string between the fixed ends.

options.text: Frequency = number of nodes x fundamental frequency
options.explanation: D. The frequency of a standing wave on a string is not equal to the number of nodes multiplied by the fundamental frequency. The frequency of a standing wave is determined by the tension in the string, the mass per unit length of the string, and the length of the string, but not by the number of nodes.

questions.explanation: A string plucked from 1/2 of the length or any of the further harmonics shows symmetry about it's midpoint, if the wave pattern is divided into two equal parts, then it is observed that they are symmetrical. The frequency of such wave is given by ω/2π.

questions.text: Beats can be used to find:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Beats can be used to find the frequency difference between two sound waves or periodic signals. When two waves of slightly different frequencies interfere with each other, they produce a phenomenon known as "beats." Beats are periodic variations in the amplitude (loudness) of the resulting sound wave, which occur due to constructive and destructive interference.The process of beat formation occurs as follows:1. Two sound waves of slightly different frequencies (f₁ and f₂) are superimposed. Let's assume f₁ > f₂.2. The waves with frequencies f₁ and f₂ combine, leading to the formation of a composite wave.3. As the two waves interfere, there are instances where the crests of one wave align with the crests of the other wave, resulting in constructive interference and an increase in amplitude (loudness) of the composite wave.4. Similarly, there are instances where the crests of one wave align with the troughs of the other wave, leading to destructive interference and a decrease in amplitude (loudness) of the composite wave.5. This periodic variation in amplitude creates the phenomenon of beats, where the loudness of the sound alternates between being stronger and weaker.The number of beats per second (B) can be calculated using the formula:B = |f₁ - f₂|where |f₁ - f₂| represents the absolute value of the frequency difference between the two waves.Beats are commonly used in tuning musical instruments. When a musician tunes an instrument, they may produce a reference tone of known frequency and then adjust the instrument until it produces a beat frequency with a tuning fork or another reference tone. When the beat frequency becomes zero (no more periodic variations in loudness), it indicates that the two frequencies are identical, and the instrument is in tune.

Beats do not directly provide information about the amplitude of waves. The amplitude of a wave refers to the maximum displacement of particles in the medium from their equilibrium position. It is typically measured using instruments such as oscilloscopes or by visual observation.

questions.explanation: Applying the phenomenon of beat unknown frequency, ‘n’ of a tuning fork can be measured. A tuning fork of known frequency is chosen by trial and error, which produces beat with this tuning fork. If the known frequency of the tuning fork is n1, then the unknown frequency is almost equal to ‘n’.Now the number of beats in is counted. According to the definition, m = n1 – n. Then a small amount of wax is attached to any arm of the tuning fork to slightly decrease the frequency in order to find whether n1 is greater or smaller than ‘n’. If the number of beats increases then n1 is greater than ‘n’, hence m = n1 – n or, n1 = n + m. But if the number of beats decreases, then m = n – n1 or, n, = n – m. In this way, the unknown frequency of the tuning fork can be determined.

questions.text: In a stationary wave, the distance between a node and consecutive antinode is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a stationary wave, the distance between a node and the consecutive antinode is equal to one-fourth (1/4) of the wavelength (λ) of the wave.A stationary wave, also known as a standing wave, is formed by the superposition of two waves of the same frequency and amplitude traveling in opposite directions. The interference of these waves results in nodes (points of minimum displacement) and antinodes (points of maximum displacement) along the wave.The pattern of nodes and antinodes in a stationary wave is fixed and depends on the boundary conditions of the wave medium. For a stationary wave on a string fixed at both ends (like a guitar string or a vibrating rope), the first harmonic or fundamental mode has one node and two antinodes. The distance between a node and its adjacent antinode is one-fourth of the wavelength.Mathematically, the relationship between the wavelength (λ) and the distance between a node and consecutive antinode (d) can be expressed as:d = λ / 4This relationship holds true for any harmonic or mode of vibration in a stationary wave on a string fixed at both ends. For higher harmonics, the number of nodes and antinodes increases, but the distance between a node and consecutive antinode remains one-fourth of the wavelength.

The interference of these waves results in nodes (points of minimum displacement) and antinodes (points of maximum displacement) along the wave.The pattern of nodes and antinodes in a stationary wave is fixed and depends on the boundary conditions of the wave medium. For a stationary wave on a string fixed at both ends (like a guitar string or a vibrating rope), the first harmonic or fundamental mode has one node and two antinodes. questions.explanation: The approximate distance between a node and the immediate next antinode is actually 'one-fourth of a given wavelength'.In other words, the total distance between 2 consecutive nodes or antinodes is 'half of the wavelength produced'.

questions.text: Ten complete waves pass through a point in 2 seconds. If the wavelength is 20 cm, what is the speed of the wave?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The speed (v) of a wave can be calculated using the formula:v = λ / Twhere:v = Speed of the waveλ = Wavelength of the waveT = Time period for ten complete waves to pass through a pointGiven:Wavelength (λ) = 20 cm = 0.20 m (since 1 m = 100 cm)Time period (T) for ten complete waves = 2 secondsNow, let's calculate the speed of the wave:v = 0.20 m / 2 sv = 0.10 m/sThe speed of the wave is 0.10 meters per second (m/s).

questions.explanation: Frequency=10/2=5HzWavelength=0.20mVelocity= frequency x wavelength=1m/s

questions.text: A longitudinal wave is moving through a medium. Which of the following statements about the direction of the propagation of the wave and displacement of the medium is true?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Displacement of medium = parallel to the energy transfer Direction of the propagation of wave = parallel to the energy transfer
options.explanation: Longitudinal waves are the waves in which the displacement of the particles/medium is parallel to the direction of propagation of wave/direction of energy transfer.We know that waves transfer energy and a wave will always transfer energy in the direction of its propagation.Hence Option A is the correct option.

options.text: Displacement of medium = parallel to the energy transfer Direction of the propagation of wave = perpendicular to the energy transfer
options.explanation: Displacement of medium = parallel to the energy transfer and Direction of the propagation of wave = perpendicular to the energy transfer is incorrect because this describes a transverse wave, not a longitudinal wave. In a transverse wave, the displacement of the medium is perpendicular to the direction of propagation of the wave.

options.text: Displacement of medium = perpendicular to the energy transfer Direction of the propagation of wave = parallel to the energy transfer
options.explanation: Displacement of medium = perpendicular to the energy transfer and Direction of the propagation of wave = parallel to the energy transfer is also incorrect. This does not describe a longitudinal wave.

options.text: Displacement of medium= perpendicular to the energy transfer Direction of the propagation of wave = perpendicular to the energy transfer
options.explanation: Displacement of medium = perpendicular to the energy transfer and Direction of the propagation of wave = perpendicular to the energy transfer is also incorrect because this describes a different type of wave called a surface wave.

questions.explanation: Option A) Longitudinal waves are the waves in which the displacement of the particles/medium is parallel to the direction of propagation of wave/direction of energy transfer. We know that waves transfer energy and a wave will always transfer energy in the direction of its propagation. Hence Option A is the correct option.Option B) Displacement of medium = parallel to the energy transfer and Direction of the propagation of wave = perpendicular to the energy transfer is incorrect because this describes a transverse wave, not a longitudinal wave. In a transverse wave, the displacement of the medium is perpendicular to the direction of propagation of the wave.Option C) Displacement of medium = perpendicular to the energy transfer and Direction of the propagation of wave = parallel to the energy transfer is also incorrect. This does not describe a longitudinal wave.Option D) Displacement of medium = perpendicular to the energy transfer and Direction of the propagation of wave = perpendicular to the energy transfer is also incorrect because this describes a different type of wave called a surface wave.
hints: direction of the propagation of the wave

questions.text: The shortest distance between two points on the wave that have a phase difference of (π/3) is 5 cm. What is its wavelength?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as per formulaPhase difference= (2π)/(wavelength) × (distance between the two points)Phase difference = 2π/λ xdistance between two pointsπ/3 = 2п/λ × 5π/3 = 10π/λ1/3 = 10/λ3 = λ/10λ = 30 cmIt is a numerical and it can have only one possible answer.

questions.explanation: Phase difference= (2π)/(wavelength) x (distance between the two points) Phase difference = 2π/λ x distance between two points π/3 = 2π/λ x 5 π/3 = 10π/λ 1/3 = 10/λ 3 = λ/10 λ = 30 cm

questions.text: Which of the following frequencies of sound waves is audible?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option does not lie in audible range; hence, the option is incorrect
5000 Hz lies in the audible range, and hence the option is correct.
2500 kHz is above the audible range; hence, the option is incorrect
50 kHz is also above the audible range. Hence, the option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Humans can detect sounds in a frequency range from about 20 Hz to 20 kHz. (Human infants can actually hear frequencies slightly higher than 20 kHz but lose some high-frequency sensitivity as they mature; the upper limit in average adults is often closer to 15–17 kHz.)
